:::{#def-model-error-noise}
#### Model deviation from the population mean

The **model deviation from the population mean**
in a probabilistic model $p(Y)$,
is the difference between an observed value $y$ and its population mean:

$$\devn(y) \eqdef y - \Expf{Y}$$ {#eq-def-model-error}
:::

:::{.notes}
We use the same notation for this model deviation
that we used for [deviations](probability.qmd#def-deviation-pop-mean):
$\devn(y)$.
Many sources call this quantity "error" or "noise."
In this course,
we reserve the term "error" for deviations of estimates
from their estimands
([estimation error](estimation.qmd#def-estimation-error)).

$\Expf{Y}$ can be viewed as an estimate of $Y$, before $y$ is observed.
Conversely, each observation $y$ can be viewed as an estimate of $\Expf{Y}$
(albeit an imprecise one, individually, since $n=1$).

:::

We can rearrange @eq-def-model-error
to view $y$ as
the sum of its mean plus the model deviation
(often called error/noise):

$$y = \Expf{Y} + \devn(y)$$

:::{#def-signal}
#### Signal (statistical sense)

In statistical modeling,
the **signal** is the deterministic part of the model.
For mean-based models,
the signal is the model-implied mean function,
for example $\E{Y \mid X}$ (or $\E{Y}$ when there are no covariates).
:::
